title: He Ran His First Marathon and Did the Impossible!
summary: Imagine running 26.2 miles faster than anyone thought possible—and still not winning! That's what happened to Yomif Kejelcha at the London Marathon. He finished in 1 hour, 59 minutes, and 41 seconds. That's under the magical 2-hour mark that runners have dreamed about for years. But here's the twist: he came in second! Sabastian Sawe of Kenya was even faster, setting a new world record. Kejelcha, 28, wasn't sad at all. "I'm very, very happy because I broke two hours," he said. It was his first-ever marathon, and he didn't think he could do it. Before the race, he guessed he'd finish around 2 hours and 2 or 3 minutes. But during the race, he and Sawe pushed each other. With one mile left, Sawe zoomed ahead. Kejelcha looked at his watch near the finish and couldn't believe his eyes. He was way under 2 hours! Another runner, Jacob Kiplimo, also beat the old world record but came in third. Kejelcha says he'll try again, and since he was only 11 seconds behind, he thinks he can break 2 hours again. Sometimes, losing can still feel like winning.
why_it_matters: This story shows that even if you don’t win, reaching a personal goal can feel just as amazing—like beating your own best time in a school race.

questions.question: What was Yomif Kejelcha's finishing time?
How did Kejelcha feel after the race?
How many seconds behind the winner was Kejelcha?
What time did Kejelcha predict he would run before the race?

background_read: The London Marathon is a famous 26.2-mile race held every year in England since 1981. Yomif Kejelcha is a runner from Ethiopia; Sabastian Sawe is from Kenya. For a long time, people thought a marathon could never be finished in under two hours—it was like a magic barrier.
Article_Structure: WHO: Yomif Kejelcha, a 28-year-old Ethiopian running his first marathon, and Sabastian Sawe, a Kenyan runner who won the race.
WHAT: Kejelcha finished in 1:59:41, breaking the two-hour barrier, but Sawe was even faster and set a new world record. Another runner, Jacob Kiplimo, also beat the old record.
WHY: Sawe zoomed ahead with one mile left, but Kejelcha was still thrilled because he achieved a dream he didn’t think possible.

perspectives.description: He is overjoyed because he broke the 2-hour mark in his very first marathon, a huge personal achievement he never thought possible.
He is proud to win and set a new world record, pushing himself to be the fastest man in the race.
They are amazed to see such fast times and that even the runner who didn’t win made history by crashing through an almost impossible barrier.
